title: React Basics
description: Components, props, state, hooks. Build modern UIs with the most popular library.

questions.q: What is React?
options: A JavaScript library for building user interfaces using a component-based architecture
A full-stack framework with built-in database
questions.explanation: React is intentionally a 'library' (just the UI layer). Frameworks like Next.js add routing, SSR, and conventions on top.

questions.q: Who originally created React?
questions.explanation: React was open-sourced in 2013 and has dominated frontend development ever since. Meta still maintains it actively.

questions.q: What problem does React PRIMARILY solve?
options: Efficiently keeping the UI in sync with changing application state through a component model
questions.explanation: Before React, mutating the DOM imperatively was error-prone at scale. React's declarative model + diffing made complex UIs manageable.

questions.q: What is the 'Virtual DOM' in React?
options: An in-memory representation of the UI that React diffs against the previous version to update the real DOM efficiently
A backup copy of HTML files
questions.explanation: React builds a Virtual DOM tree, compares it to the previous tree (reconciliation), and applies the minimum necessary real DOM changes.

questions.q: Which is TRUE about React?
options: It is declarative — you describe what the UI should look like for a given state, not how to update it
It is imperative — you manually update DOM elements
questions.explanation: Declarative thinking is the React mindset shift. You return 'the UI for this state'; React figures out the updates.

questions.q: What is JSX?
options: A syntax extension that lets you write HTML-like markup directly inside JavaScript
questions.explanation: JSX is syntactic sugar that compiles to React.createElement() calls. Optional but used in ~99% of React codebases.

questions.q: Why is `className` used instead of `class` in JSX?
options: `class` is a reserved word in JavaScript, so JSX uses `className` to avoid conflict
questions.explanation: Same reason `for` becomes `htmlFor`. JSX attributes follow camelCase JavaScript naming conventions, not HTML conventions.

questions.q: How do you embed a JavaScript expression in JSX?
questions.explanation: Single curly braces enclose any JS expression — variable, function call, ternary, arithmetic. Statements (if, for) are NOT allowed.

questions.q:
What does this JSX render? `
{2 + 3}
`
questions.explanation: JS expressions inside JSX evaluate first, then render. Useful for conditional rendering, calculations, and string interpolation.

questions.q: Why must a JSX component return ONE root element (or a Fragment)?
options: A function can only return one value — multiple sibling elements need a wrapper or <> </> Fragment
questions.explanation: Use <> ... </> (Fragment) when you don't want an extra wrapper DOM element. Available since React 16.2.

questions.q: What is a React component?
options: A reusable function that returns JSX describing a piece of UI
questions.explanation: Components are the fundamental unit of React. Compose them like LEGO bricks to build entire applications.

questions.q: Which is a VALID React functional component?
questions.explanation: Functional components are just JavaScript functions returning JSX. The dominant pattern since React Hooks (2019).

questions.q: Why must React component names start with a CAPITAL letter?
options: JSX uses capitalization to distinguish custom components from native HTML tags
questions.explanation: <Button> renders your custom Button component; <button> renders the HTML element. Capitalization is meaningful, not stylistic.

questions.q: Which is the DIFFERENCE between class and functional components today?
options: Functional components with Hooks are the modern standard; class components are legacy
Class components are required for state
Class components only work in mobile
questions.explanation: Since 2019, functional components with Hooks do everything class components can do, with less boilerplate. Classes are maintained for legacy.

questions.q: What is component COMPOSITION?
options: Building complex UIs by combining smaller components — the React way to reuse code
questions.explanation: Composition over inheritance is a core React principle. A Page contains a Header, Sidebar, and Content — each a component.

questions.q: What are 'props' in React?
options: Read-only inputs passed from a parent component to a child component
questions.explanation: Props (short for properties) are how parents configure children. Treat props as immutable inside the receiving component.

questions.q: How do you pass a prop to a component?
questions.explanation: Props look like HTML attributes. String values use quotes; JS values use curly braces: <Greeting count={5} />.

questions.q: How does the receiving component access its props?
options:
As a parameter: function Greeting({ name }) { return

Hi {name}

; }
questions.explanation: Destructuring props in the parameter list is the modern convention. Older code uses function Greeting(props) { props.name }.

questions.q: Why are props READ-ONLY?
options: Modifying props would break React's data flow assumptions and cause unpredictable bugs
Props are stored in a database
questions.explanation: Data flows down (parent to child). To change shared data, lift state up to the common parent and pass new props down.

questions.q: What does this prop spread do? `<Button {...props} />`
options: Passes all properties of the props object as individual props to Button
questions.explanation: Spread is handy but be careful — you can accidentally pass unwanted DOM attributes or override important props.

questions.q: What is 'state' in React?
options: Data that can change over time within a component and triggers a re-render when updated
questions.explanation: State is mutable data owned by a component. When state changes, React re-renders the component with the new value.

questions.q: How do you declare state in a functional component?
questions.explanation: useState returns a pair: the current value and a setter function. Destructure it on declaration.

questions.q: Why must you use the setter function (setCount) instead of `count = 5` to update state?
options: Direct mutation doesn't trigger a re-render — React needs the setter to know state changed
questions.explanation: React tracks state via the setter call. Direct assignment makes the new value invisible to React's reconciliation.

questions.q: What's the SAFE way to update state based on the previous value?
options: setCount(prev => prev + 1) — use the function form
questions.explanation: The function form guarantees you operate on the latest state, avoiding stale-closure bugs in batched updates.

questions.q: What happens when state changes in a React component?
options: React schedules a re-render of that component (and its children)
questions.explanation: Re-render means React calls the component function again with the new state, computes new JSX, diffs, and updates the real DOM.

questions.q: How do you conditionally render content in JSX?
questions.explanation: JSX accepts JS expressions, not statements. Use && for show/hide, ternary for either/or, or extract logic to a helper function.

questions.q:
What's the BUG in this code? `{count &&

Count: {count}

}`
options: When count is 0, React renders '0' instead of nothing (0 is falsy but renderable)
questions.explanation:
Common gotcha. Use {count > 0 &&

...

} or {!!count &&

...

} to avoid showing '0' on the page.

questions.q: Which is a CLEAN way to render different UIs based on a status?
options: A switch statement OR an object map: const views = { loading: <Spin/>, error: <Err/> }; return views[status];
if statements outside JSX with no return
questions.explanation: Object maps and early returns produce much cleaner code than nested ternary expressions.

questions.q: How do you EARLY RETURN to skip rendering?
options: if (!user) return null;  before the main return statement
questions.explanation: Returning null tells React to render nothing for this component. Returning undefined or omitting return is an error.

questions.q: Which approach AVOIDS deeply nested conditional JSX?
options: Extract conditional logic into separate components or helper functions
questions.explanation: When conditions get complex, that's a signal to compose smaller components. Each does one thing.

questions.q: How do you render a list of items in JSX?
options: Use a for loop inside JSX
questions.explanation: .map() transforms an array of data into an array of JSX. JSX accepts arrays of elements.

questions.q: Why does React require a `key` prop on list items?
options: Keys help React efficiently identify which items changed, were added, or removed between renders
Keys make the list look nicer
questions.explanation: Without stable keys, React may incorrectly reuse DOM elements, causing visual bugs and lost component state.

questions.q: What's a BAD key for list items?
options: The array index — when items can be reordered, added, or removed
questions.explanation: Index keys are OK for static lists. They cause subtle bugs for dynamic lists — use stable unique IDs whenever possible.

questions.q: Where does the `key` prop go on a list item?
options: On the outermost element returned in each iteration
questions.explanation: Each top-level element in a map() result needs a key. React reads it before rendering — it's not accessible via props.

questions.q: What happens if you forget to add keys to a list?
options: React warns in the console; bugs may appear when items are reordered or removed
The list won't render at all
questions.explanation: Always check the browser console for 'Each child in a list should have a unique key prop' warnings.

questions.q: How do you attach a click handler in JSX?
questions.explanation: React event names use camelCase (onClick, onChange). The value must be a function reference (not a function call).

questions.q: What's the BUG in `<button onClick={handleClick()}>Click</button>`?
options: The function is CALLED immediately on render instead of on click — should be onClick={handleClick}
questions.explanation: Common beginner mistake. Pass the function reference, not the result of calling it. Use an arrow if you need arguments: onClick={() => handleClick(id)}.

questions.q: How do you pass an ARGUMENT to an event handler?
options: Wrap it in an arrow: onClick={() => handleClick(id)}
questions.explanation: The arrow creates a new function that calls handleClick with id when clicked. Pre-binding (.bind) is the older pattern.

questions.q: What is the React 'synthetic event'?
options: A wrapper around the browser's native event that normalizes behavior across browsers
An event that didn't really happen
questions.explanation: React's SyntheticEvent works identically across browsers. e.preventDefault(), e.target, e.stopPropagation() all work as expected.

questions.q: How do you handle FORM input changes?
options: Read the input value after submit
questions.explanation: Controlled inputs: state drives the input value, onChange updates state. Predictable and easy to validate.

questions.q: What is useEffect for?
options: Running side effects (data fetching, subscriptions, manual DOM ops) after the component renders
questions.explanation: Side effects = anything that touches the world outside React (network, timers, DOM, third-party libraries).

questions.q: What does the dependency array do? `useEffect(fn, [count])`
options: Re-runs the effect only when items in the array change between renders
Passes count as an argument to fn
Forces the effect to run forever
questions.explanation: Empty array [] = run once after first render. No array = run after every render. [count] = run when count changes.

questions.q: What does an EMPTY dependency array `[]` mean?
options: The effect runs once after the initial mount (like componentDidMount)
The effect runs on every render
questions.explanation: Useful for one-time setup like fetching initial data or subscribing to an event. Watch out for stale closures over state/props.

questions.q: How do you CLEAN UP inside useEffect (e.g., remove an event listener)?
options: Return a cleanup function from the effect: useEffect(() => { ...; return () => cleanup(); })
questions.explanation: The returned function runs before the next effect AND on unmount. Always clean up timers, subscriptions, and event listeners.

questions.q: What's a COMMON useEffect mistake?
options: Forgetting a dependency, causing stale state/props inside the effect
questions.explanation: ESLint's react-hooks/exhaustive-deps rule catches these. Always include every prop/state used inside the effect.

questions.q: What are React Hooks?
options: Functions starting with 'use' that let you tap into React features (state, effects, context) from functional components
questions.explanation: Introduced in React 16.8 (2019). Hooks replaced class components for nearly all new React code.

questions.q: What are the RULES of Hooks?
options: Only call them at the top level (no loops/conditions) and only from React functions
Call them inside event handlers only
questions.explanation: Hooks track state by call order. Conditional calls would scramble the order. The ESLint plugin enforces this rule.

questions.q: Which is NOT a built-in React hook?
questions.explanation: Built-ins: useState, useEffect, useContext, useReducer, useRef, useMemo, useCallback, useLayoutEffect, useId, useTransition.

questions.q: What is a 'custom hook'?
options: A function starting with 'use' that combines built-in hooks to package reusable stateful logic
questions.explanation: Custom hooks let you share logic between components without HOCs or render props. e.g., useLocalStorage, useFetch, useDebounce.

questions.q: Why must custom hook names start with 'use'?
options: React's linter relies on the prefix to enforce the Rules of Hooks
questions.explanation: The 'use' prefix tells the linter (and humans) 'this is a hook, treat it accordingly'.

questions.q: What is useRef PRIMARILY for?
options: Holding a mutable value that doesn't trigger re-renders, or referencing a DOM element
questions.explanation: Two use cases: (1) imperative DOM access (focus, scroll), (2) mutable values you don't want to cause re-renders (timer IDs).

questions.q: How do you reference a DOM element with useRef?
options: const inputRef = useRef(null); <input ref={inputRef} />; then inputRef.current.focus()
questions.explanation: Standard pattern for imperative DOM operations like focus(), scroll(), play()/pause() on media elements.

questions.q: Does changing `ref.current` trigger a re-render?
options: No — that's a key difference from useState
questions.explanation: Use refs when you need to store something across renders WITHOUT triggering re-renders. Perfect for timer IDs, prev values, etc.

questions.q: What's a COMMON misuse of useRef?
options: Using it for data that SHOULD trigger UI updates — that data belongs in state
questions.explanation: If the UI needs to reflect a value, it's state. If you just need to remember something between renders without re-rendering, it's a ref.

questions.q: When does React assign the ref to a DOM element?
options: After the DOM is committed — meaning inside useEffect or event handlers, not during render
questions.explanation: Accessing ref.current during render returns null. Use it in useEffect for DOM measurements or in event handlers.

questions.q: What does the Context API solve?
options: Passing data through many levels of components without prop-drilling
questions.explanation: Great for global-ish data: theme, current user, locale. Avoid for frequently-changing values (causes re-renders everywhere).

questions.q: How do you create a context?
questions.explanation: createContext returns an object with .Provider and .Consumer. The argument is the default value.

questions.q: How do you PROVIDE a context value?
questions.explanation: Every descendant of the Provider can access the value. Re-renders trigger when value changes (use a stable reference!).

questions.q: How does a component CONSUME a context value?
questions.explanation: useContext is the modern hook-based way. Components re-render whenever the context value reference changes.

questions.q: What's a DOWNSIDE of overusing Context?
options: Any change re-renders ALL consumers — splitting contexts or using state libraries (Zustand, Redux) may be better
questions.explanation: Context is for low-frequency global data. For complex state, Zustand/Redux/Jotai offer fine-grained reactivity.

questions.q: What is a CONTROLLED input in React?
options: An input whose value is driven by React state (value + onChange)
An input wrapped in a try/catch
questions.explanation: Controlled = single source of truth. Easier to validate, transform, and integrate with the rest of your state.

questions.q: What's the difference between controlled and UNCONTROLLED inputs?
options: Uncontrolled inputs manage their own state internally; you read the value via a ref when needed
questions.explanation: Uncontrolled is fine for simple cases (file inputs, integrating with non-React code). Controlled is the React-y default.

questions.q: How do you HANDLE a form submission in React?
options: Use a regular HTML form submission
questions.explanation: e.preventDefault() stops the default page reload. Then handle the data with state or pass to a fetch call.

questions.q: Why use a form library like react-hook-form for complex forms?
options: Validation, error handling, performance, and less boilerplate for multi-field forms
questions.explanation: React Hook Form, Formik, and TanStack Form are popular choices. For 2-3 fields, plain useState is fine.

questions.q: What's the BEST way to handle form state for many fields?
options: A single state object: const [form, setForm] = useState({ name: '', email: '' });
One useState per field is always best
questions.explanation: A single object is cleaner for many related fields. Update with spread: setForm(prev => ({ ...prev, [name]: value })).

questions.q: What's the SIMPLE way to fetch data on component mount?
questions.explanation: Empty dependency array = run once after mount. Always handle loading, error, and success states properly.

questions.q: What states does a typical data-fetching component track?
options: loading, error, data — the three states of any async operation
questions.explanation: Loading spinners, error messages, and success rendering are all expected UX. Plan for all three from day one.

questions.q: Why is React Query (TanStack Query) popular for data fetching?
options: It handles caching, refetching, deduplication, and loading/error states with minimal boilerplate
It's the only way to fetch data
questions.explanation: TanStack Query (or SWR) is the modern standard. Eliminates 90% of the boilerplate of raw useEffect + useState.

questions.q: What's a COMMON useEffect fetching bug?
options: Not handling the case where the component unmounts before the fetch resolves (potential memory leak)
questions.explanation: Use AbortController to cancel pending requests on unmount, or check a 'cancelled' flag before calling setState.

questions.q: What is 'race condition' in React fetching?
options: A new fetch's response arriving AFTER an older fetch's response, causing stale data to overwrite fresh data
questions.explanation: Common when user changes filters/search rapidly. Cancel old requests (AbortController) or check request IDs before updating state.

questions.q: When does a React component RE-RENDER?
options: When its state changes, its props change, or its parent re-renders
questions.explanation: Understanding this is foundational. Most React perf bugs are unnecessary re-renders triggered by changing props/parents.

questions.q: What does React.memo do?
options: Wraps a component to skip re-renders when its props haven't changed (shallow comparison)
questions.explanation: Useful for expensive children with stable props. Don't slap it on every component — premature optimization.

questions.q: What is useMemo for?
options: Caching the result of an expensive calculation between renders
questions.explanation: useMemo(() => heavyCalc(data), [data]). Only re-runs when data changes. Use when calculations are actually expensive.

questions.q: What is useCallback for?
options: Memoizing a function reference so child components don't re-render when the parent re-renders
questions.explanation: Pairs with React.memo'd children. Without useCallback, every parent render creates a new function = new prop = child re-renders.

questions.q: What is the React Compiler (introduced in 2024)?
options: An optimizing compiler that auto-memoizes components, reducing the need for manual React.memo/useMemo/useCallback
questions.explanation: React Compiler analyzes your code at build time and adds memoization automatically. The future of React performance.

questions.q: Why doesn't React include routing out of the box?
options: React is a UI library, not a framework — routing is a separate concern handled by libraries or frameworks
questions.explanation: Use React Router for SPAs, or Next.js / Remix / TanStack Router for framework-level routing.

questions.q: What is React Router?
options: The most popular client-side routing library for React SPAs
questions.explanation: React Router (now v6+) handles URL → component mapping, nested routes, navigation, and dynamic params.

questions.q: What is a 'Single Page Application' (SPA)?
options: A web app that loads one HTML page and uses JS to dynamically update content without full page reloads
An app with exactly one page
A page with a single feature
An app that's only on one server
questions.explanation: SPAs feel app-like — no flash between pages. Drawback: heavier initial load, weaker default SEO.

questions.q: What is the difference between React Router and Next.js routing?
options: React Router is client-side; Next.js routing is filesystem-based with SSR/SSG built in
questions.explanation: Next.js auto-routes based on /app or /pages folder structure and renders server-side by default. Different paradigm.

questions.q: How do you navigate programmatically in React Router?
questions.explanation: useNavigate is the hook for programmatic navigation (e.g., after a form submit). For simple links, use <Link to='/about'>.

questions.q: When do you NEED a state management library beyond useState?
options: When many distant components need to share and update the same data
Never — useState is enough for any app
questions.explanation: useState is fine for local component state. Cross-cutting global state (user, theme, cart) benefits from a state library.

questions.q: What is Zustand?
options: A small, fast state management library with a minimal API — no boilerplate, no providers
questions.explanation: Zustand has surpassed Redux as the popular choice for new projects. Tiny API, great DX, no Provider wrapping required.

questions.q: What problem does Redux historically solve?
options: Predictable centralized state management with strict patterns (actions, reducers, store)
questions.explanation: Redux dominated 2016-2020. Still widely used in enterprise. Modern alternatives (Zustand, Jotai, Redux Toolkit) reduce boilerplate.

questions.q: What is TanStack Query for?
options: Server state — caching, syncing, and updating data fetched from APIs
questions.explanation: Big insight: server state ≠ client state. Use Query for the former, Zustand/useState for the latter. They complement each other.

questions.q: Which is a HEALTHY state management strategy?
options: useState for local, Context for theme/auth, TanStack Query for server data, Zustand for complex global UI state
questions.explanation: Right tool for the job. Most apps don't need a single all-encompassing state library.

questions.q: What happens if you call a hook inside an `if` statement?
options: React throws an error or warns — hooks must be called in the same order every render
questions.explanation: Hooks rely on call order. Conditional calls break that. The ESLint plugin react-hooks/rules-of-hooks catches this.

questions.q: Why is mutating state directly (state.push(item)) a BUG?
options: React compares references — mutating doesn't create a new reference, so React doesn't detect the change
questions.explanation: Always create new objects/arrays: setItems([...items, item]). This is core to React's update detection.

questions.q: What's the FIX for an infinite re-render loop with useEffect?
options: Check the dependency array — a non-stable reference (object/array/function) is recreated every render
questions.explanation: Objects/arrays/functions get new references each render. Wrap them in useMemo/useCallback or restructure to compare primitives.

questions.q: What does React's Strict Mode do in development?
options: Intentionally double-invokes effects and renders to surface bugs from impure code or missing cleanup
questions.explanation: Strict Mode catches side effects and stale closures in dev. Production behavior is unchanged. Keep it on; fix what it surfaces.

questions.q: Why should event handlers be defined OUTSIDE the JSX return?
options: For readability and to easily wrap in useCallback if needed — but inline arrows are fine for simple cases
questions.explanation: Both work. Extract complex handlers for clarity. Inline arrows are fine for one-liners — premature optimization is the enemy.

questions.q: What is Vite?
options: A fast modern build tool and dev server used to scaffold React (and other) projects
questions.explanation: Vite replaced Create React App as the recommended starter for SPAs. Lightning-fast HMR and lean output.

questions.q: What is Next.js?
options: A React framework with file-system routing, SSR, SSG, API routes, and image optimization
questions.explanation: Next.js is the default for production React apps that need SEO, performance, or backend logic in one repo.

questions.q: What is Tailwind CSS?
options: A utility-first CSS framework commonly used with React (className-based styling)
questions.explanation: Tailwind dominates modern React styling for many teams. Pairs well with shadcn/ui components.

questions.q: What is shadcn/ui?
options: A set of beautifully designed React components you COPY into your project (not an npm install)
questions.explanation: shadcn/ui ships source code to your repo. You own and modify it. Built on Radix primitives + Tailwind. Huge in 2024-2026.

questions.q: What is TypeScript's role in modern React?
options: Adds static typing to props, state, and APIs — catches bugs at compile time before they reach production
questions.explanation: Most serious React projects use TypeScript. The DX with typed props and IntelliSense is dramatically better than plain JS.

questions.q: What are React Server Components (RSC)?
options: Components that render on the server, never ship to the client, and can directly access databases/APIs
Components hosted on a remote server
A new type of class component
questions.explanation: RSC is React's biggest architectural shift in years. Default in Next.js App Router; reduces client JS bundle size dramatically.

questions.q: What's the DIFFERENCE between Server Components and Client Components?
options: Server Components run only on the server; Client Components are interactive and run in the browser (marked with 'use client')
Server components are faster but identical
questions.explanation: Mental model: render data on server (RSC); add interactivity at the leaves with 'use client' components.

questions.q: What does the 'use client' directive do?
options: Marks a file/component as client-side — it runs in the browser and can use state, effects, event handlers
questions.explanation: By default in App Router, components are Server. Add 'use client' at the top of a file to opt into client behavior.

questions.q: What are React Server Actions?
options: Functions marked 'use server' that run on the server and can be called from client components — used for mutations
questions.explanation: Server Actions enable form submissions and mutations without writing API routes. Type-safe RPC built into React/Next.

questions.q: What's the BEST way to keep up with React in 2026 and beyond?
options: Follow the official React blog, build projects, read React Docs (react.dev), follow core team on X/Twitter
questions.explanation: React evolves fast. The new docs (react.dev) and the React blog are the canonical sources of truth.
